title: Fundamental plane (spherical coordinates)

infobox.value: This [elementary geometry](/wiki/Geometry "Geometry")-related article is a [stub](/wiki/Wikipedia:Stub "Wikipedia:Stub"). You can help Wikipedia by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Fundamental_plane_(spherical_coordinates)&action=edit).

in a [spherical coordinate system](/wiki/Spherical_coordinate_system "Spherical coordinate system") is a [plane of reference](/wiki/Plane_of_reference "Plane of reference") that divides the [sphere](/wiki/Sphere "Sphere") into two [hemispheres](/wiki/Sphere "Sphere"). The [geocentric latitude](/wiki/Geocentric_latitude "Geocentric latitude") of a [point](/wiki/Point_(geometry) "Point (geometry)") is then the [angle](/wiki/Angle "Angle") between the fundamental plane and the line joining the point to the centre of the sphere.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Rogers, Lucy (2008), [*It's ONLY Rocket Science: An Introduction in Plain English*](https://books.google.com/books?id=JTFGAAAAQBAJ&pg=PA136), Springer, p. 136, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780387753782](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780387753782 "Special:BookSources/9780387753782").

Newcomb, Simon (1906). [*A Compendium of Spherical Astronomy with Its Applications to the Determination and Reduction of Positions of the Fixed Stars*](https://archive.org/details/acompendiumsphe00newcgoog). Macmillan. p. [92](https://archive.org/details/acompendiumsphe00newcgoog/page/n116)..
["NASA - Besselian Elements of Solar Eclipses"](https://eclipse.gsfc.nasa.gov/SEcat5/beselm.html). *NASA*. Retrieved 13 August 2022.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Plane of reference that divides the sphere into two hemispheres

For the concept in extragalactic astronomy, see [Fundamental plane (elliptical galaxies)](/wiki/Fundamental_plane_(elliptical_galaxies) "Fundamental plane (elliptical galaxies)").

The **fundamental plane** in a [spherical coordinate system](/wiki/Spherical_coordinate_system "Spherical coordinate system") is a [plane of reference](/wiki/Plane_of_reference "Plane of reference") that divides the [sphere](/wiki/Sphere "Sphere") into two [hemispheres](/wiki/Sphere "Sphere"). The [geocentric latitude](/wiki/Geocentric_latitude "Geocentric latitude") of a [point](/wiki/Point_(geometry) "Point (geometry)") is then the [angle](/wiki/Angle "Angle") between the fundamental plane and the line joining the point to the centre of the sphere.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

For a [geographic coordinate system](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system") of the Earth, the fundamental plane is the [Equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator").

[Astronomical coordinate systems](/wiki/Astronomical_coordinate_system "Astronomical coordinate system") have varying fundamental planes:[[2]](#cite_note-2)

* The [horizontal coordinate system](/wiki/Horizontal_coordinate_system "Horizontal coordinate system") uses the observer's [horizon](/wiki/Horizon "Horizon").
* The [Besselian coordinate system](/w/index.php?title=Besselian_coordinate_system&action=edit&redlink=1 "Besselian coordinate system (page does not exist)") uses Earth's [terminator](/wiki/Terminator_(solar) "Terminator (solar)") (day/night boundary).[[3]](#cite_note-3) This is a [Cartesian coordinate system](/wiki/Cartesian_coordinate_system "Cartesian coordinate system") (*x*, *y*, *z*).
* The [equatorial coordinate system](/wiki/Equatorial_coordinate_system "Equatorial coordinate system") uses the [celestial equator](/wiki/Celestial_equator "Celestial equator").
* The [ecliptic coordinate system](/wiki/Ecliptic_coordinate_system "Ecliptic coordinate system") uses the [ecliptic](/wiki/Ecliptic "Ecliptic").
* The [galactic coordinate system](/wiki/Galactic_coordinate_system "Galactic coordinate system") uses the [Milky Way](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way")'s galactic equator.
